1:
The chapter opens with Jesus leaving Galilee for "the coasts of Judaea beyond Jordan," and the witnesses read the move on two levels — its historical route and its redemptive symbolism. The grammatical-historical voices map the geography. Barnes and Ellicott identify this as the start of the Peraean ministry (Luke 9:51 to Luke 18:30), Jesus crossing the Jordan to avoid Samaria on his last journey to Jerusalem; Robertson notes the Hebraism "it came to pass," a formula Matthew uses to close major blocks of teaching (Matthew 7:28; Matthew 13:53). Clarke, citing Josephus, argues the route ran "by the side of Jordan," not beyond it. Henry (puritan-evangelical) marks the solemnity: this was Christ's "final farewell" to Galilee, for "Christ did not take his leave of Galilee till he had done his work there" — a comfort to faithful ministers, who "are not removed from any place till they have finished their testimony" there (Revelation 11:7).

The Antiochene Chrysostom (twice) explains the timing: having long avoided Judaea "on account of the envy of those men, now He frequents it, because the passion was to be nigh at hand," yet healing rather than only teaching, "that by His miracles faith might be given to what He said." The Alexandrian Origen reads the healings "beyond Jordan, where baptism was given," as a figure: "all are truly healed from spiritual sickness in baptism," yet he distinguishes the crowds who merely "followed" from Peter and Matthew, who "arose and followed" — "few are they who have arisen and followed, who also, in the regeneration, shall sit on twelve thrones" (anticipating v.28).

The Latin fathers add allegory. Raban distinguishes "Judaea proper" (Judah and Benjamin) from the wider land; the Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary, looking ahead to the divorce question, warns that "when you see either man or woman enquiring concerning divorce, know that that man is lustful and that woman unchaste." Remigius hears in "a man shall leave father and mother" the mystery of Ephesians 5:32: "the Lord Jesus Christ left His Father when He came down from heaven... and clave unto His wife, that is, the Holy Church."

Hilary reads the healing of Galileans on Judaea's border as admitting "the sins of the Gentiles to that pardon which was prepared for the Jews." Wesley simply notes Christ "walked no more in Galilee." The witnesses thus agree: this geographical turn toward the cross is also a turn that keeps the Gentiles in view, and the healings authenticate the teaching that follows.
2:
This brief transitional verse — "And great multitudes followed him; and he healed them there" — draws only short comment, the voices being grammatical and devotional. Gill fills in the geography and the motive: the crowds came "not only from Galilee, but from the adjacent parts, from the country beyond Jordan, and the coasts of Judea, where he had been formerly," and Christ, "according to his usual manner," both "taught and instructed them in the knowledge of divine things, and confirmed his doctrines by miracles." The healing was not isolated wonder-working but the seal upon his teaching.

Barnes simply refers back to his note on v.1, treating the verse as continuous with the account of the journey. Clarke offers the one analytical touch, distinguishing the mixed motives of the throng: "Some to be instructed — some to be healed — some through curiosity — and some to ensnare him." The same crowd that received his mercy contained those who, as the next verse shows, came only to lay a trap.

Wesley keeps it briefest of all, noting that Christ "healed them there — that is, wheresoever they followed him," underscoring that his compassion was not confined to one place but went with him along the whole road. The thinness of the witnesses' comment matches the brevity of the verse, which the evangelist gives as a hinge between the departure from Galilee (v.1) and the controversy over divorce that occupies the heart of the chapter (vv.3-12).

Taken together, the witnesses see in this single line the steady pattern of Christ's Peraean ministry: wherever he went, the needy followed, and wherever they followed, he healed. The verse offers no debate and no controversy, only the quiet record of a mercy that moved with him, and that drew to him a multitude as varied in motive as humanity itself — the earnest, the curious, the suffering, and the hostile alike gathered around the same Healer. It is against this backdrop of free and abundant healing that the Pharisees' captious question in v.3 stands out in sharp and ungracious relief.

The brevity of comment here is itself a kind of testimony. Where the voices have little to add, they do not pad the text with invented meaning but let the plain fact stand: the same compassion shown in Galilee followed Christ across the Jordan, undiminished by the journey and unhindered by the gathering hostility of his enemies. The healing of "great multitudes" in the very region where he would soon be questioned and, beyond it, betrayed, quietly testifies that his mercy was not deterred by the cross drawing near. The reader is meant to carry this picture — of a Saviour freely healing all who came — into the controversies that follow, so that the hardness of the Pharisees and the wealth of the young ruler are measured against the open generosity of the One they failed to receive.
3:
The Pharisees approach "tempting him": "Is it lawful for a man to put away his wife for every cause?" The witnesses agree the question was a snare, and the grammatical-historical voices set it in its rabbinic context. Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, and Lightfoot all explain the dispute between the schools of Shammai (divorce only for unchastity) and Hillel (divorce "for any passing whim," as Robertson puts it, "if the husband saw a prettier woman or burnt his biscuits for breakfast"). Whatever Christ answered would offend one party or the people. Ellicott adds the political danger: in Peraea, the territory of Herod Antipas, who had himself divorced the daughter of Aretas, "the Herodian party were working with the Pharisees," hoping Jesus would "provoke the tetrarch's wrath, as the Baptist had done"; he also notes Josephus, who divorced two wives "on grounds comparatively trivial."

The puritan-evangelical Henry gives the fullest exposition. Christ "would not give the rule without a reason," and proves the indissoluble union of husband and wife by three things: the creation of one Eve for one Adam (Genesis 1:27); the law that "a man shall leave father and mother, and cleave to his wife" — a bond "nearer than that between parents and children"; and the nature of the contract, "they twain shall be one flesh," so that to divorce is to "put away a piece of himself" (Malachi 2:15). Hence "what God hath joined together, let not man put asunder."

The Alexandrian Origen notes that those who came "to tempt Him" received in reply not evasions but "dogmas." The Reformed Calvin states the abiding fruit: "by means of this occurrence... a fixed law was laid down as to the sacred and indissoluble bond of marriage." The Pharisees calculated that either answer would be "offensive" — abolishing the Law, or making Christ "a pander" to lust — "but the Son of God... disappointed them," reducing the question to two heads: "that the order of creation ought to serve for a law," and that Moses tolerated divorce "not because [it was] lawful, but because... he had to deal with a rebellious and intractable nation."

Gill identifies the questioners as spies, and Wesley glosses "for every cause" as "for any thing which he dislikes in her — this the scribes allowed." The witnesses thus speak as one: a malicious question becomes the occasion for Christ to restore marriage to its creation pattern.
4:
Christ answers the trap by appeal to creation: "Have ye not read, that he which made them at the beginning made them male and female?" The witnesses note that he replies not from the rival rabbinic schools but from Moses himself. Barnes: "Instead of referring to the opinions of either party, Jesus called their attention to the original design of marriage, to the authority of Moses — an authority acknowledged by them both." God "at the beginning made but one man and one woman," that their posterity "should learn that the original intention of marriage was that a man should have but one wife." Thus "he answered the question, not from Hillel or Shammai... but from Moses, and thus defeated their malice."

Clarke draws the principle for every age: "To answer a case of conscience, a man should... pay no regard to that which the corruption of manners has introduced into Divine ordinances, but go back to the original will, purpose, and institution of God. Christ will never accommodate his morality to the times." Ellicott observes that "the answer to the question is found not in the words of a code of laws, but in the original facts of creation," which "represented the idea of man and woman as created for a permanent relationship... not as left to unite and separate as appetite or caprice might prompt." Gill notes Christ refers them "to the original creation of man, and to the first institution of marriage, previous to the law of Moses."

The Reformed Calvin and Wesleyan Wesley press the polemic against polygamy. Calvin: Christ "insists on the number two, as the prophet Malachi (Malachi 2:15)... and thus from the order of creation is proved the inviolable union of one husband with one wife"; even the death of a first wife does not multiply wives, for "the second wife is substituted by God in the room of the first, as if she had been one and the same woman." Wesley sharpens it: "By making them one man and one woman, he condemned polygamy: by making them one flesh, he condemned divorce." The witnesses converge without dissent: the creation order, not human custom, is the standard, and it establishes monogamy and permanence together.
5:
"For this cause shall a man leave father and mother, and shall cleave to his wife: and they twain shall be one flesh." The witnesses dwell on the authority and the depth of these words from Genesis 2:24. The grammatical-historical voices note a textual nicety: though Adam spoke the words, Christ ascribes them to God. Ellicott: "words so uttered, prompted by the Holy Spirit, and stamped with the divine sanction, might well be looked on as an oracle from God." Gill cites the rabbi Jarchi, who likewise calls them the words of "the holy Spirit." Robertson observes the Hebraism in "the twain shall become one flesh," where the Septuagint follows the Hebrew idiom directly. Clarke unfolds "cleave" as a "beautiful metaphor": the union is "more intimate and binding than even paternal or filial affection... a well-glued board will break sooner in the whole wood than in the glued joint"; and "one flesh" means not only one body but "two souls in one body," and the offspring in whom the two literally become one.

The Reformed Calvin draws his argument "from the less to the greater": "the bond of marriage is more sacred than that which binds children to their parents. But piety binds children to their parents by a link which cannot be broken. Much less then can the husband renounce his wife." He guards against misreading: "it was not the design of Christ to introduce the impure and filthy speculation of Plato," but to honour "the order which God has established" — the husband ruling "so as to be the head, and not the tyrant, of his wife," the wife yielding "modestly to his commands." Like Wesley, he hears the condemnation of polygamy: "the mixture of three or four persons is unauthorized."

The Alexandrian Origen lifts the verse to its apostolic mystery: since Paul applies "they twain shall be one flesh" to Christ and the Church (Ephesians 5:31-32), "the Lord — the husband — left the Father whom He saw when He was in the form of God" and "for the sake of the church" became one with her; the synagogue, by contrast, "committed fornication... and slew Him." Wesley simply notes the words were spoken "by the mouth of Adam, who uttered them." The witnesses agree: the marriage bond, rooted in creation and sealed as one flesh, is stronger than every other natural tie, and dimly images the union of Christ and his Church.
6:
"Wherefore they are no more twain, but one flesh. What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder." The witnesses gather around the closing maxim, reading it as the divine prohibition of all arbitrary divorce. The grammatical-historical voices weigh the words. Robertson stresses the pronoun: "Note 'what,' not 'whom.' The marriage relation God has made," and "joined together" means "yoked together," a "timeless aorist... true always" — "the creation of sex, and the high doctrine as to the cohesion it produces... interdict separation." Ellicott observes that "the essence of the marriage is made to depend, not on laws, or contracts, or religious ceremonies, but on the natural fact of union," and that "the evil of every system that multiplies facilities for divorce is that it treats as temporary what was designed to be permanent." Clarke expands the metaphor of the yoke at length, recalling the ancient gem of Cupid and Psyche, both "winged," "veiled," and bound by "a chain of pearls" — emblem that "the union is precious, beautiful, and delightful."

Barnes simply cross-references 1 Corinthians 7:10, the apostolic echo of the Lord's command. The Reformed Calvin reads the verse as restraining "the caprice of husbands": Christ "declares that it is not in the power of the husband to dissolve the marriage," and "forbids all others to confirm by their authority unlawful divorces; for the magistrate abuses his power when he grants permission." Every tempted husband should reflect, "Who art thou that allowest thyself to" tear apart what God has joined? Calvin extends the principle polemically against Rome's withholding of the cup: "In the Holy Supper, Christ joined the bread and the wine; but they have dared to withhold from all the people the use of the cup."

Ellicott candidly notes that, "strictly interpreted, the words go further than those of Matthew 5:32, and appear to forbid divorce under all circumstances," but takes them as "the expression of the principle that should underlie laws," asserting "the true ideal of marriage" while the concession of vv.7-9 deals with what "violates and annuls the ideal." The witnesses agree: the union is God's act, and no human will — husband or magistrate — may rightly undo it.
7:
The Pharisees press their objection: "Why did Moses then command to give a writing of divorcement, and to put her away?" The witnesses agree the answer turns on the difference between command and permission. The grammatical-historical Barnes states the inference they drew: "if [Moses] had allowed them, they could not be unlawful" (Deuteronomy 24:1). Clarke notes the abuse of Scripture by "the impure and unholy" who "seek for a justification of their conduct from the law of God itself," recalling a man who read the Bible chiefly for "those places that spoke of... the polygamy of the patriarchs." Ellicott hears the question coming "from the advocates of the laxer school," who "fell back from what would seem to them a vague abstract principle upon the letter of the Law."

The Alexandrian Origen frames the deeper question — whether in such laws "we are to seek nothing beyond the letter" — and answers that Moses "for your hardness of heart suffered you to put away your wives; but from the beginning it hath not been so."

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest analysis. The Pharisees "had thought of this calumny, to have it all ready," reasoning "that whatever God permits by his law... is lawful." But Christ "disarms the falsehood," replying "that Moses permitted it on account of their obstinacy, and not because he approved of it as lawful." Strictly, "he did not permit it; but in so far as he did not strictly forbid it, he is said to have permitted it." The law of divorce "was made solely for the protection of the women, that they might not suffer any disgrace after they had been unjustly rejected" — "rather a punishment inflicted on the husbands than an indulgence." Calvin then distinguishes "political and outward order" from "spiritual government": civil laws may connive at many things "for which every man's conscience reproves him," yet the magistrate must not "make this an excuse for his indolence," nor private men "screen their own vices under the protection of the laws."

Gill identifies the questioners as hoping to "ensnare him," and Wesley supplies the key correction: "Christ replies, Moses permitted (not commanded) it, because of the hardness of your hearts." The witnesses converge: what the Pharisees called a command was a concession to human sin, framed to limit cruelty, not to license lust.
8:
"Moses because of the hardness of your hearts suffered you to put away your wives: but from the beginning it was not so." The witnesses unfold the contrast between concession and creation. The grammatical-historical voices fasten on the substituted verb. Ellicott: the force lies "in emphasized substitution of 'suffered' for 'commanded,'" since "the scribes of the school of Hillel had almost turned divorce into a duty"; and in "the grounds assigned" — "but for the possibility of divorce the wife would have been the victim of the husband's tyranny; and law, which has to deal with facts, was compelled to choose the least of two evils." He draws a wide principle: the same "hardness of heart" explains "whatever else in the Law of Moses... deviates from the standard of eternal righteousness," and warns that "where a nation calling itself Christian has sunk so low... a concessive legislation may be forced upon the State even while the churches assert their witness of the higher truth." Robertson notes the perfect tense, "it hath not been so": "The original ordinance has never been abrogated nor superseded, but continues in force."

Barnes explains the national reference: "He speaks here of his hearers as a part of the nation. The hardness of you Jews" — as when "we say, we fought with England," meaning our fathers. Clarke warns of the slide by which "toleration... raises itself insensibly into permission, and permission soon sets up for command," and reads "from the beginning" as "In Bereshith it was not so." Lightfoot adds a striking note: "hardness of heart for the most part in Scripture denotes rather obduration against God than against men" — Israel given over "to spiritual fornication, that is, to idolatry" (Acts 7:42) — so the phrase carries "something that savours of punishment."

The Alexandrian Origen generalizes: "some of the laws were written not as excellent, but as by way of accommodation to the weakness of those to whom the law was given," and finds even in "the new covenant... some legal injunctions of the same order" — Paul's concessions in 1 Corinthians 7 "because of our weakness." The witnesses agree: the Mosaic toleration was a remedy for sin, never the divine ideal, which stands unrepealed "from the beginning."
9:
"Whosoever shall put away his wife, except it be for fornication, and shall marry another, committeth adultery." Here Christ "set forth the Law with authority" (Chrysostom), and the witnesses debate the scope of the exception and the remarriage clause. The Latin and Alexandrian fathers stress the gravity of the one ground. Jerome: "it is fornication alone which destroys the relationship of the wife," who "has divided one flesh into two"; the prohibition of remarriage "while the first is yet alive" guards against false accusation and against the adulteress losing all "fear of disgrace." Origen probes whether other grave crimes might also dissolve the bond, but holds to the text. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary observes that "it is not matrimony but the will that makes the union... it is not a separation of bodies but a separation of wills that dissolves it." Augustine notes that "a reunion of the wedlock, even after actual commission of adultery, is neither shameful nor difficult, where there is... remission of sin."

The grammatical-historical voices weigh the legal force. Barnes emphasizes the "I": "this he proclaimed to be the law of his kingdom... Only one offence was to make divorce lawful." Ellicott notes that here, more emphatically than in Matthew 5:32, the husband "by contracting another marriage commits adultery"; the utmost Christ allows is "a divorce, a mensa et thoro, not a vinculo," and points to Paul's handling of further cases in 1 Corinthians 7:10-15. Robertson defends the genuineness of the exception against critics like McNeile who think it a later addition opposed to the context: "Why not, if Shammai on this point agreed with Jesus?... Jesus has lifted the whole subject of marriage and divorce to a new level, far beyond the petty contentions of the schools."

The Reformed Calvin insists the right is mutual — "he is not the lord of his body" — so that a wife is freed by her husband's adultery as he by hers; and he rejects the notion that the innocent party must remain unmarried, since Christ "evidently grant[s] permission in this case." Wesley reads it as a revocation of the old indulgence: "I revoke that indulgence from this day." The witnesses divide over whether the innocent may remarry — most of the fathers stress the bond's permanence, while Calvin and Robertson allow the innocent party's remarriage — but all agree Christ has raised marriage far above the casuistry of the schools.
10:
The disciples recoil: "If the case of the man be so with his wife, it is not good to marry." The witnesses note both their carnal motive and Christ's measured reply. The Latin fathers read the saying with sympathy and caution. Jerome frames the burden — "a wife is a grievous burden, if it is not permitted to put her away except for the cause of fornication" — and insists the gift of continence is "given to those who have sought it of God." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary answers their despair: "the prize is before them; he who desires the honour will not consider the toil... None would ever vanquish, if all shunned the struggle," yet "unless we receive the aid of grace, we have not strength." Hilary distinguishes the threefold cause — "nature... violence... and his own choice." The Alexandrian Origen, like the rest, teaches "that absolute chastity is a gift given by God, and not merely the fruit of training."

The grammatical-historical voices catch the disciples' worldliness. Ellicott: "the laxer view of the school of Hillel was the more popular one even with" the disciples, who "looked forward to the possible discomforts of marriage" and concluded its disadvantages "outweighed its advantages"; he memorably notes that "one of the greatest of English writers has taken the same line of thought" — Milton's Doctrine and Discipline of Divorce being "but an elaborate and eloquent expression of the words of the disciples." Robertson observes that Christ's doctrine "was too high even for the Twelve." Clarke, deriving "husband" from "house-band, the bond of the house," laments that the disciples set "a flat contradiction to the decision of the Creator," who said "it is not good for man to be alone."

The Reformed Calvin rebukes their selfishness: "devoted to themselves and their own convenience," they "reject a wonderful gift of God" from "the dread or dislike of a single inconvenience"; and he turns sharply on Jerome for "loading with calumnies that sacred and divinely appointed condition of life," gathering "terms of reproach from profane authors." Whatever annoyances belong to marriage "are accidental, for they arise out of the depravity of man... marriage began to be a medicine." The witnesses agree: the disciples' conclusion springs from the flesh, and Christ will answer it not by despising marriage but by defining the rare gift of celibacy (vv.11-12).
11:
"All men cannot receive this saying, save they to whom it is given." The witnesses agree that Christ neither endorses the disciples' rash conclusion nor condemns marriage, but points to a special gift. Barnes identifies "this saying" with the disciples' words "that it was good for a man not to marry": it "might be good in certain circumstances, in times of persecution and trial, or for the sake of labouring in the cause of religion... but it was not given to all men" (1 Corinthians 7:1, 7, 9). Ellicott agrees the reference is to the disciples' comment, not the divorce rule: "There were but few who were capable of acting safely on that conclusion. For those who were not so capable... marriage, with all its risks, was the truer, healthier, safer state."

Clarke draws the balance: "Neither of the states is condemned. If thou marry, thou dost well... But if thou do not marry (because of the present necessity, persecution, worldly embarrassments, or bodily infirmity), thou dost better" (1 Corinthians 7:25). Robertson stresses the "voluntary renunciation of marriage for the sake of the kingdom," "a direct appeal to the spiritual intelligence of the disciples not to misconceive his meaning as certainly the monastic orders have done."

The Reformed Calvin makes the decisive point against self-imposed celibacy: "the choice is not placed in our hands, as if we were to deliberate on a matter submitted to us. If any man... lays upon himself an obligation to celibacy, he is widely mistaken. God, who has declared it to be good that a man should have a woman to be his helper, will punish the contempt of his own appointment." The gift of continence "is a special gift; for when he says 'to whom it is given,' he plainly shows that it was not given to all" — "this reproves the pride of those who do not hesitate to claim for themselves what Christ so manifestly refuses to them."

Wesley distinguishes "three sorts of persons" to whom the gift is given — "by natural constitution... by violence... and by grace with their choice" (anticipating v.12). The witnesses speak with one voice: celibacy is no universal counsel and no human achievement, but a particular gift of God, and those without it ought not to forsake the marriage he ordained.
12:
Christ names "three kinds of eunuchs" — born so, made so by men, and those "which have made themselves eunuchs for the kingdom of heaven's sake." The witnesses agree the last is spiritual, not bodily. The Latin fathers set the pattern. Jerome distinguishes "two carnal and one spiritual," and warns that "none of them obtain the kingdom of heaven, save he only who has become a eunuch for Christ's sake"; the saying "He that is able to receive it" is "the voice of the Lord exhorting... that he who can fight might fight and conquer." Chrysostom is emphatic that Christ "does not mean cutting off of members, but a putting away of evil thoughts. For he that cuts off a limb is under a curse... opens a door to Manichaeans"; true peace comes not from "amputation of a member" but from "a bridle upon the thoughts."

The grammatical-historical voices identify the historical analogue. Barnes and Clarke both point to the Essenes, who "held that marriage was unsuitable to their condition," perpetuating their sect "by adopting the poor children of others." Clarke recalls with horror that "the great Origen, understanding the latter clause... literally — O human weakness! — went, and literally fulfilled it on himself." Ellicott reads the words as teaching that only "those who are in some sense eunuchs... can safely abstain from marriage," for "the celibacy of self-indulgence, or even of selfish prudence, tends but too fatally to impurity"; the celibacy must be "for the kingdom of heaven's sake," and "the Church of Rome and the founders of monastic orders were not wrong in their ideal... Their mistake lay in enforcing that ideal as a rule on those who had not the power to realise it."

The Reformed Calvin agrees the spiritual eunuchs "have castrated themselves, that they may be more at liberty to serve God," and presses that "celibacy is not a virtue" in itself, "not in itself more pleasing to God than fasting," but must "have a reference to another object" — freedom "for being employed in better things for the glory of God." He laments the "deadly cord of a vow" by which "wretched souls were bound." Gill and Wesley round it off: the gift is for the few, "Happy they! who have abstained... that they might walk more closely with God." The witnesses converge: voluntary celibacy is a spiritual self-denial for the kingdom's sake, never a meritorious mutilation or a vow to be imposed on the unwilling.
13:
"Then were there brought unto him little children, that he should put his hands on them, and pray; and the disciples rebuked them." The witnesses divide their attention between the act of blessing and the disciples' fault, and several draw the link to infant baptism. The Latin fathers connect the scene to the foregoing teaching. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary notes that, after discourse "of chastity," some brought infants, "who in respect of chastity are the purest," and rebukes the disciples: "who were worthy to come to Christ, if simple infancy were thrust away?" Jerome stresses Christ's wording, "Of such is the kingdom of heaven, not Of these, to shew that it was not years, but disposition." Hilary reads "the infants are a type of the Gentiles, to whom salvation is rendered by faith and hearing." The Alexandrian Origen reads the babes mystically as those "yet carnal in Christ, having need of milk," and warns the advanced disciple not to "despise the little ones of the Church."

The grammatical-historical and puritan voices fasten on the disciples' rough indifference. Robertson: "It is a tragedy to make children feel that they are in the way at home and at church... The new child world of today is due directly to Jesus." Lightfoot argues the children were brought by believing parents "that he might... admit them into his discipleship, and mark them for his by his blessing" — "the infants of believers were as much disciples and partakers of the kingdom of heaven as their parents." Henry draws out the parents' faith ("those who glorify Christ by coming to him themselves should further glorify him by bringing all they have"), the disciples' fault ("Christ has more love and tenderness in him than the best of his disciples"), and the children's place: "the children of believing parents belong to the kingdom of heaven, and are members of the visible church... The promise is to you, and to your children."

The Reformed Calvin makes the scene a shield against the Anabaptists: Christ "receives not only those who... freely approach to him, but those who are not yet of age to know how much they need his grace," and "could not present the infants solemnly to God without giving them purity." Clarke laments that "this consecration of children to God seems to have grown out of use." The witnesses agree: Christ's welcome of the children rebukes every barrier set between them and his grace.
14:
"Suffer little children, and forbid them not, to come unto me: for of such is the kingdom of heaven." The witnesses hear Christ's indignant tenderness and his claim for the children. Ellicott notes that Mark adds Jesus "was much displeased," reproducing "almost verbally the teaching of Matthew 18:3": "The tenderness of His sympathy was kindled into indignation at the rough indifference of the disciples," his love for children springing "from His seeing in them the children of His Father." "Of such is the kingdom" means "the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these, is theirs as by inheritance." Robertson parses the imperatives sharply: "'Leave them alone'... 'Stop hindering them.'"

Barnes takes "the kingdom of heaven" as "the church," and the lesson as character: "Whosoever shall not be humble, unambitious, and docile, shall not be a true follower of Christ"; he adds the careful hope that "it is greatly to be hoped that all infants will be saved. No contrary doctrine is taught in the sacred Scriptures." Clarke is more vehement, finding here that which "utterly ruins the whole inhuman diabolic system of what is called non-elect infants' damnation; a doctrine which must have sprung from Moloch." Christ "loves little children because he loves simplicity and innocence; he has sanctified their very age by passing through it himself."

The Reformed Calvin again turns the verse against the Anabaptists: Christ "embraces" and "blesses" the children, "from which we infer that his grace is extended even to those who are of that age"; "to exclude from the grace of redemption those who are of that age would be too cruel," and "since baptism is the pledge and figure of the forgiveness of sins... it ought not to be denied to" infants "whom God adopts." Yet Calvin balances it: "under this term he includes both [infants] and those who resemble them," for "no man [enters] unless he be made to resemble a child" — though "not children in understanding, but in malice" (1 Corinthians 14:20). Wesley reads "of such" as belonging to "little children, either in a natural or spiritual sense." The witnesses agree: the kingdom is for the childlike, and the children themselves are not to be shut out from the Saviour who claims them.
15:
"And he laid his hands on them, and departed thence." The closing act of the scene draws brief comment, but the witnesses do not let it pass without weight. Gill reports that "he put his hands upon them, according to the custom of the country, and wished all kind of prosperity to them," and that Mark records he "blessed them." Barnes is briefest: "he pronounced or sought a blessing on them." Clarke turns to the geography, noting that Christ "departed thence — that is, from that part of Judea which was beyond Jordan (Matthew 19:1); and then went to Jericho" (Matthew 20:29), so that the blessing of the children stands as the last recorded act before the journey resumes.

The fullest comment is Ellicott's, who, like the Church of England's Baptismal Office, finds here "the true warrant for infant baptism." St. Mark, he observes, "records, as before, the act of caressing tenderness: 'He folded them in His arms, and laid His hands upon them.'" These acts, he argues, weigh "more than doubtful passages in the Acts and Epistles; more than the authority, real or supposed, of primitive antiquity; more than the legal fiction that they fulfil the condition of baptism by their sponsors." They "justify the Church of Christ at large in commending infants, as such, to the blessing of their Father." His decisive question follows: "The blessing and the prayer of Christ cannot be regarded as a mere sympathising compliance with the fond wishes of the parents, and if infants were capable of spiritual blessings then, why... should they be thought incapable now?"

The thinness of the witnesses' comment matches the single verse, yet Henry (under v.13) had already drawn the note the others assume — that "when he had done this, he departed thence," as if "he reckoned he had done enough there, when he had thus asserted the rights of the lambs of his flock, and made this provision for a succession of subjects in his kingdom." The witnesses agree: the imposition of hands was a real bestowal of blessing, not an empty gesture, and the children went away truly blessed of Christ.
16:
"Good Master, what good thing shall I do, that I may have eternal life?" The witnesses divide over the questioner's character even as they agree on the lesson. The Latin fathers lean toward suspicion: Jerome calls him "young, rich, and proud," asking "not as one that desires to learn, but as tempting Him." But the Antiochene Chrysostom defends him: "I cannot consider him to have been a hypocrite... Mark removes all suspicion, for he says that Jesus when He looked on him, loved him"; rather, "great is the tyranny of wealth." Raban distinguishes "the Active" life ("Thou shalt not kill") from "the Contemplative" ("If thou wilt be perfect"). Remigius notes Christ "set out to him the precepts of the Law" and bade him "sell ALL," restoring unjust gains but selling lawful possessions. Origen even cites "the Gospel according to the Hebrews," where the rich man "began to scratch his head," reproved because brethren "perish for hunger" while "thy house is full."

The puritan Henry treats him generously: "not a better question could be asked, not more gravely." "Good Master" bespeaks submissiveness; the man "had a firm belief of eternal life" and "was concerned to make it sure." Christ's answer "tenderly assists his faith" — "Why callest thou me good?" leading him "to own and honour him as a good God" — and "plainly directs his practice": "If thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments," for "eternal life is the only true life," entered "by obedience" as the evidence of a title held by Christ's merit.

The grammatical-historical Barnes identifies him as "a ruler" who "had lived externally a blameless life, but yet he was not at peace," depending "on his works." Clarke admires that "he came running... he kneeled... he came in the spirit of a disciple." Ellicott ventures a striking conjecture — "What if the young ruler were Lazarus himself?" — noting the wealth, the "ruler of the Jews," and the verbal echo of "One thing is needful" (Luke 10:42). The Reformed Calvin reads him as one who "dreams of merits," whom "a blind confidence in his works hindered" — "Christ appropriately sends him to the keeping of the law, which... is the way of righteousness." Wesley notes drily, "Many of the poor had followed him from the beginning. One rich man came at last." The witnesses agree: a sincere but self-trusting seeker is being led, step by step, to see that he cannot save himself.
17:
"Why callest thou me good? there is none good but one, that is, God... but if thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments." The witnesses labour to show that Christ neither disclaims deity nor teaches salvation by works. The grammatical-historical Barnes reads it as a reproof of flattery: "Why do you give to me a title that belongs only to God?... a most severe reproof of a foolish habit of compliment"; Christ "did not intend here to disclaim Divinity," but to correct the man's loose use of words and to test him, since "he had by no means kept the commandments." Clarke records the important variant — "Why dost thou question me concerning that good thing?" with "There is one who is good" — adopted by Griesbach on weighty authority, and shows that even the older anti-Trinitarian reading "destroys the whole doctrine built on this text" against Christ's Godhead. Ellicott likens Christ's method to that "of Socrates... in the grave, sad irony of the process," designed to deepen the man's thought of goodness "until they rose to Him in whom alone it was absolute."

The Reformed Calvin rejects the refined reading that Christ here hints at his divinity; rather "Christ had no other intention than to maintain the truth of his doctrine... Thou falsely callest me good, unless thou acknowledgest that I have come from God." On the commandments, Calvin gives the classic exposition: "Christ did not take into consideration what men can do, but replied to the question, What is the righteousness of works?" The keeping of the law "is righteousness, by which any man who kept the law perfectly... would obtain life"; but "as we are all guilty, nothing but cursing will be found in the law," so the law is "the first part of instruction, by which we are led to the righteousness which is obtained by faith" (Romans 10:5-6). Thus "this reply of Christ is legal," meant to convince the man "of his weakness, that he might betake himself to the assistance of faith."

Wesley guards against irony: "Our Lord therefore does not answer ironically, which had been utterly beneath his character, but gives a plain, direct, serious answer... Believe, and thence love and obey. And this undoubtedly is the way to eternal life." Robertson sums the Antiochene-grammatical point: "One there is who is good, one alone who is really good in the absolute sense." The witnesses converge: Christ directs the self-confident inquirer to the law's perfect standard precisely to drive him, in his failure, to grace.
18:
"He saith unto him, Which?" — and Christ recites the commandments of the second table. The witnesses note both the selection and its order. Lightfoot observes the pattern: "how again and again in the New Testament, when mention is made of the whole law, only the second table is exemplified" (Romans 13:8-9; James 2:8, 11), "charity towards our neighbour" being "the top of religion, and a most undoubted sign of love towards God." Barnes enumerates the commandments cited — the sixth, seventh, eighth, ninth, fifth, and the love of neighbour (Leviticus 19:18) — expounding each, and noting Mark's added "Defraud not."

Ellicott explains the method. To a different questioner Christ "would have pointed (as in Matthew 22:37) to the two great commandments"; but here "it was more in harmony with His loving purpose to leave out of sight altogether the commandments of the first table... and to direct attention only to those which... were thought common and familiar things." The reordering — the fifth following rather than preceding the others — "seems to imply a design to lead the seeker through the negative to the positive forms of law, through definite prohibitions of single acts to the commandments which were exceeding broad."

The Reformed Calvin gives the rationale: "though Christ intended to show that we are bound to obey the whole law, he should mention the second table only... because from the duties of charity the disposition of every man is better ascertained." Piety toward God "holds, no doubt, a higher rank; but as the observation of the first table is often feigned by hypocrites, the second table is better adapted for making a scrutiny." He also corrects an error: that the fifth commandment "is declared to belong to the second table, that no one may be led astray by the error of Josephus, who thought that it belonged to the first."

Clarke adds the gospel caveat against legalism: "we are not to keep these commandments in order to purchase eternal life... To save your souls, Christ must save you from your sins." Gill asks "Which?" with the man, wondering whether moral or ceremonial. The witnesses agree: Christ names the plain duties to one's neighbour, both because love of neighbour proves the heart and because the man must be brought, through the "broad" commandments, to see his own lack.
19:
"Honour thy father and thy mother: and, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself." The witnesses comment briefly, the verse completing Christ's recital of the second table. Gill notes that the fifth commandment, "the first with promise" and "the first of the second table," is "here mentioned last," but that this inversion "is of no consequence," citing the rabbinic maxim "there is neither first nor last in the law" — "it is of no consequence which commandment is recited first, or which last."

The capstone is "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself" (Leviticus 19:18), and Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) offers the fullest reflection, rehabilitating a much-abused phrase: "Self-love, as it is generally called, has been grievously declaimed against... But they have not understood the subject." The vicious propensity wrongly called self-love "might be more properly termed self-hatred or self-murder." True self-love, "in the sense in which our Lord uses it," is "a disposition essential to our nature... by which we desire to be happy"; rightly governed, "he who is wholly governed by self-love... will devote his whole soul to God." But self-love cannot itself make us happy, for the soul is "only the subject which receives the happiness"; God is "the object that constitutes this happiness." Thus "he that sinneth against God wrongeth his own soul... and is so far from being governed by self-love that he is the implacable enemy of his best and dearest interests in both worlds."

Barnes simply anchors the citation in Leviticus 19:18. Cyril of Jerusalem (Eastern), here as elsewhere in the chapter, sets the command in his catechesis, urging that we "honour also the fathers of our flesh" while honouring our heavenly Father, obeying earthly parents "in all things" except where they oppose godliness — for "He that curseth father or mother, let him die the death." Wesley merely cross-references Exodus 20:12. The witnesses, though brief, agree: the love of neighbour, summed in the golden standard "as thyself," is the heart of the second table and the test the young ruler claimed, mistakenly, to have met.

The placing of "honour thy father and thy mother" beside "love thy neighbour as thyself" is itself instructive, for the witnesses see in it the movement from the nearest human duty to the widest. The honour owed to parents is the first school of charity, where a child learns to prefer another's good to his own; and the love of neighbour extends that same disposition to all. Thus the second table, which the young ruler boasted he had kept, is not a checklist of avoided crimes but a single demand of self-giving love, reaching from the family outward to every person God sets in our path. Measured against this standard — love of neighbour "as thyself," with all that Clarke shows it to involve — the man's confident "All these have I kept" (v.20) will prove, under Christ's gentle probing, to be the claim of one who had never truly weighed what the commandment required.
20:
"All these things have I kept from my youth up: what lack I yet?" The witnesses read the reply as self-ignorance mixed with genuine unease. The grammatical-historical voices weigh the wording. Clarke notes the verbal difference: Christ said "keep" (tērēson), "earnestly, diligently, as with watch and ward," while the young man says, more modestly, "all these (ephylaxa) have I observed... I have kept them in custody"; yet "he felt a troubled conscience, and a mind unassured of the approbation of God." Ellicott hears "a tone of impatient surprise": "He had come seeking some great thing... He finds himself re-taught the lessons of childhood, sent back, as it were, to a lower form in the school of holiness. He had not learnt that to keep any one of those commandments in its completeness is the task of a life." Yet Ellicott finds hope in the very question: "his condition was not that of the self-satisfied Pharisee. The question implied a dissatisfaction with himself, a sense of incompleteness, as hungering and thirsting after a higher righteousness."

Robertson names the contradiction precisely: "Here is a psychological paradox. He claims to have kept all these commandments and yet he was not satisfied. He had an uneasy conscience... He thought of goodness as quantitative (a series of acts) and not qualitative (of the nature of God)." His question, Robertson judges, revealed "proud complacency or pathetic despair? A bit of both most likely."

Barnes supplies the man's meaning: "Is there anything that I lack — any new commandments to be kept? Do you, the Messiah, teach any new commands... which it is proper for me to obey, in order to be saved?" Wesley grants the claim only on the surface: "So he imagined; and perhaps he had, as to the letter; but not as to the spirit, which our Lord immediately shows." Gill notes that to be "so early serious and religious, amidst so much riches and grandeur," was "both remarkable and commendable," even if only "externally."

The witnesses converge: the young ruler's confidence was sincere but shallow, resting on the letter of the law while blind to its spirit; and his restless "What lack I yet?" is the opening through which Christ will reach his heart in the next verse.
21:
"If thou wilt be perfect, go and sell that thou hast, and give to the poor... and come and follow me." The witnesses agree this was a test aimed at the man's particular idol, not a universal rule. The grammatical-historical Barnes defines "perfect" as "complete in all its parts" — as Job was "perfect" not sinless but proportioned in piety — and reports Mark's "Jesus beholding him loved him" and "One thing thou lackest"; the command tested whether he loved God and neighbour "more than he did his property." Lightfoot sets it in the rabbinic idiom: Christ "speaks according to the idiom of the nation, which thought so," provoking the man "by the very doctrine of the Pharisees which he professed," "not that hence he either devoted Christians to voluntary poverty."

Ellicott is emphatic that the command is not binding on all: "It would be altogether a mistake to see in this either an obligation binding on all seekers... or even... a counsel of perfection. It was strictly a remedy for the special evil which hindered the young ruler's progress." Yet "so far as any one is conscious of being under the power of wealth... there is a call to some act asserting his victory," in "the spirit, if not in the letter." He notes that modern charity has "a wider range of action" — "schools or hospitals... churches... missions" — and that Mark's added "Take up thy cross, and follow Me" reveals what the seeker could not yet grasp: "Via crucis, via lucis." Robertson calls it "a huge demand, for he was rich."

The wesleyan voices stress the heart. Clarke: "his heart was too much attached" to his goods, "a continual snare"; Christ "intended to make him a preacher of his salvation." Wesley insists on the limit: "He who reads the heart saw his bosom sin was love of the world... To him therefore he gave this particular direction, which he never designed for a general rule. For him that was necessary to salvation: to us it is not." Cyril of Jerusalem (Eastern) supplies the balancing truth that wealth is not evil in itself: "Riches, and gold, and silver are not, as some think, the devil's... Do thou but use it well... money may become a door of the kingdom of heaven: Sell that thou hast, and give to the poor." The witnesses agree: Christ lays his finger on the one thing the man loved more than God, and the demand is binding on each of us only so far as we too are "in the grip of money."
22:
"But when the young man heard that saying, he went away sorrowful: for he had great possessions." The witnesses read the sad ending as the unmasking of an idol, yet with a lingering note of hope. The grammatical-historical Barnes is blunt: "He had great possessions. He was very rich. He made an idol of them. He loved them more than God. He had NOT kept the commandments from his youth up; nor had he kept them at all." Rather than "do good with his treasures, and seek his salvation by obeying God, this young man chose to turn away." Robertson: "He felt that Jesus had asked too much of him. He worshipped money more than God when put to the test"; yet "Does Jesus demand this same test of every one? Not unless he is in the grip of money. Different persons are in the power of different sins. One sin is enough to keep one away from Christ."

Clarke draws the inner agony: "Men undergo great agony of mind while they are in suspense between the love of the world and the love of their souls." The man "is now miserable even while he possesses" his goods — "And so will every soul be, who puts worldly goods in the place of the supreme God." Ellicott finds in the sorrow itself a sign: Mark's word means "frowning, or as with a look that lowered"; "the discipline so far did its work. It made the man conscious of his weakness." Yet "the sorrow... was not without an element of hope. A mere worldling would have smiled with cynical contempt, as the Pharisees did (Luke 16:14). Here there was at least a conflict." Consistent with his earlier conjecture, Ellicott suggests "the Lord, who loved the seeker... did not leave him to himself," and that "the sickness, the death, the resurrection of Lazarus, may have been the discipline" that proved "the things that are impossible with men are possible with God."

Gill summarizes the cost the man refused: "he must... become a follower of Christ, deny himself, and submit to hardships very disagreeable to the flesh." The witnesses agree: confronted with the choice between his wealth and Christ, the young ruler chose his wealth — but his very sorrow shows the choice was not made lightly, and (some hope) not made finally.
23:
"Verily I say unto you, That a rich man shall hardly enter into the kingdom of heaven." The witnesses agree the danger is in the love of riches, not riches themselves, and they wrestle with the strength of Christ's words. The Antiochene Chrysostom: Christ condemned "not riches in themselves, but those who were enslaved by them"; having said it was "hard," he proceeds "to shew that it is impossible" (v.24), then soothes the troubled disciples with "with God all things are possible." Hilary, Raban, and Remigius temper it: "to have riches is no sin; but moderation is to be observed"; "it is safer neither to have nor to love them"; Mark's "them that trust in riches" shows that the rich are saved not in their wealth but by "conversion."

The Latin fathers spin out the camel and needle (carried into v.24). The Gloss reports the "needle's eye" gate through which a camel passes only "on its bended knees, and after its burden had been taken off." Jerome softens "impossible" to "hard," recalling "the camels of Midian" bearing gifts to Jerusalem (Isaiah 60:6) — the rich entering "when they have laid aside the heavy load of sins." Gregory reads the camel as humility and the needle's eye as Christ's passion, "a needle which pricked the body with pain." Augustine notes that since the rich "are few in comparison of the multitude of the poor," the disciples "understood all who wish for riches, as included in the number of the rich."

The puritan Henry gives the sustained exposition: it is "a very hard thing for a rich man to get to heaven," for "more duties are expected from them... and more sins do easily beset them"; the difficulty is so great that it is "fitly set forth" by the camel and needle, "a difficulty altogether unconquerable by the art and power of man." Yet "though it be hard, it is not impossible," for "with God all things are possible" (Genesis 18:14) — "not that rich people should be saved in their worldliness, but that they should be saved from it."

The Reformed Calvin warns "how great an obstacle is presented by riches": though "riches do not, in their own nature, hinder us from following God," yet "in consequence of the depravity of the human mind, it is scarcely possible for those who have a great abundance to avoid being intoxicated." He notes the doctrine is "highly useful to all" — to the rich, to be on guard; to the poor, to be "satisfied with their lot." The witnesses agree: wealth is a deadly snare, and only almighty grace can free the heart it has captured.
24:
"It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God." The witnesses agree this is a proverb of impossibility, and resist attempts to soften it. The grammatical-historical voices document the saying. Lightfoot and Clarke cite the Talmudic parallel of "an elephant going through the eye of a needle," a thing "impossible," and Clarke quotes the Koran's borrowing — "till a camel shall pass through the eye of a needle." Barnes calls it "a proverb in common use among the Jews, and still common among the Arabians," denoting "the extreme difficulty of the case," not to be pressed literally; a rich man means "one who loves his riches, and makes an idol of them... while he has this feeling, it is literally impossible that he should be a Christian," though "a man may have much property, and not have this feeling."

Robertson is firm against the explainers: "Jesus, of course, means by this comparison... to express the impossible. The efforts to explain it away are jejune like a ship's cable (kamilon)" or "a narrow gorge or gate of entrance for camels... All these are hopeless, for Jesus pointedly calls the thing 'impossible' (verse 26)." Ellicott surveys and dismisses both the "cable" reading ("not a single MS. gives that reading") and the "needle's eye gate" conjecture ("could the earlier use of the term... be proved, [it] would give a certain vividness... It is not, however, necessary"). The proverb "states a general fact, the hindrance which wealth presents to the higher growths of holiness, in the boldest possible form."

The Reformed Calvin discusses the camel/cable question at length, candidly preferring "a rope used by sailors," yet the editor notes critics have "very generally abandoned" that and "returned to the true camel," for "had due allowance been made for the boldness of Eastern imagery, the supposed difficulty would have disappeared." Wesley drives it home pastorally: "Rich man! tremble! feel this impossibility; else thou art lost for ever!" The witnesses agree: the image is deliberately impossible, designed not to be tamed but to drive the rich to the only power that can save them — the grace of God (v.26).
25:
"When his disciples heard it, they were exceedingly amazed, saying, Who then can be saved?" The witnesses read the disciples' astonishment as both natural and instructive. Robertson catches the force of the imperfect: they "were literally 'struck out'" — blank amazement. Ellicott finds "an almost child-like naïveté in the question": the disciples "had found the desire of wealth to be the universal passion," and "even they themselves... had done so (as Peter's question showed, Matthew 19:27) as with a far-sighted calculation. They were counting on outward riches in that kingdom as well as outward glory." He notes that this very feeling "has affected the text," for Mark's softening clause, "How hardly shall they that trust in riches enter," is absent from the best manuscripts — likely "a marginal note, added by some one who felt as the disciples felt," that "found its way into the text."

Clarke probes the underlying assumption: "The question of the disciples seemed to intimate that most people were rich, and that therefore scarcely any could be saved." He then presses the definition that exposes us all: "A rich man... is not one who has so many hundreds or thousands more than some of his neighbours; but is one who gets more than is necessary to supply all his own wants... and keeps the residue still to himself, though the poor are starving... a man who gets all he can, saves all he can, and keeps all he has gotten." His verdict thunders: "Can such a person enter into the kingdom of God? All, No!!!"

The Reformed Calvin draws the contrast between the disciples and the young ruler: "they did not shrink from the doctrines of Christ. The case was different with him who was lately mentioned; for he was so much alarmed by the severity of the commandment, that he separated from Christ; while they, though trembling... do not break off in an opposite direction, but are desirous to conquer despair." Hence "it will be of service to us to tremble at the threatenings of God... provided that our minds are not discouraged, but rather aroused." Wesley answers the question simply: "Who? A poor man; a peasant; a beggar: ten thousand of them, sooner than one that is rich." The witnesses agree: the disciples' alarm rightly feels the weight of the saying, and their staying — unlike the ruler's leaving — is itself a mark of faith.
26:
"But Jesus beheld them, and said unto them, With men this is impossible; but with God all things are possible." The witnesses gather around the look and the promise. Ellicott reads the gaze: "He had gazed thus on the young ruler, and read his inner weakness. Now... he reads that of the disciples; and the look... tells of wonder, sorrow, tenderness, anxiety." The words, "general as they are in their form," seemed to rebuke the disciples' "hasty judgment," for "He, the Teacher, would still hope, as against hope, for one in whom He had seen so much to love." Their wider teaching is "that wealth, though bringing with it many temptations, may be so used, through God's grace, as to be a help, not a hindrance." Robertson: "Jesus saw their amazement." Wesley feels the tenderness: "O what a speaking look was there!... With the utmost sweetness... he does not retract what he had said... but rather strengthens it, by representing the salvation of a rich man as the utmost effort of Omnipotence."

Clarke states the doctrine: "God alone can take the love of the world out of the human heart. Therefore the salvation of the rich is represented as possible only to him: and... it requires more than common exertions of Omnipotence to save a rich man."

The Reformed Calvin draws the pastoral and polemical point. Christ "does not entirely free the minds of his disciples from all anxiety; for it is proper that they should perceive how difficult it is to ascend to heaven," that they may "distrust themselves" and "implore strength from heaven." Far from extenuating the danger, "he now affirms it to be impossible"; thus "those teachers are guilty of gross impropriety, who are so much afraid to speak harshly, that they give indulgence to the slothfulness of the flesh." And the verse "refutes that widely embraced principle — which the Papists have borrowed from Jerome — 'Whoever shall say that it is impossible to keep the law, let him be accursed.' For Christ plainly declares that it is not possible for men to keep the way of salvation, except so far as the grace of God assists them."

Gill notes Christ "knew their reasonings among themselves, though they did not speak out." The witnesses agree: what is utterly beyond human power — the saving of a heart enslaved to wealth — is the very work that the omnipotent grace of God delights to perform.
27:
"Behold, we have forsaken all, and followed thee; what shall we have therefore?" The witnesses weigh Peter's question — its boldness, its smallness, and its flaw. The Antiochene Chrysostom asks, "What was this 'all,' O blessed Peter? The reeds, your net, and boat." Yet Peter speaks "not to call to mind his own magnanimity, but in order to propose the case of the multitude of poor" — that no poor man, having nothing, should think himself unable to be perfect. The Latin fathers echo this: Jerome notes Peter added "and have followed thee," which "did the philosopher Crates" not do — the following "is peculiar to the Apostles and believers." The Alexandrian Origen casts Peter as "a victorious athlete" asking "what are the prizes of his contest," and out of "the fulness of their love they had so forsaken those least things, as they would have forsaken the greatest."

The grammatical-historical voices are candid about both the poverty and the flaw. Barnes: "Probably nothing but their fishing-nets, small boats, and cottages. But they were their all; their living, their home. And, forsaking them, they had as really shown their sincerity as though they had possessed the gold of Ophir." Clarke: "'A poor all,' says one, 'a parcel of rotten nets.' No matter — they were their all," and "that man forsakes much who reserves nothing to himself." Yet Ellicott and Robertson detect the self-interest: the question "betrayed the thoughts that had been working in the minds of the disciples"; they "had complied with the letter only, not with the spirit, of the command... dwelling on what they had done, as in itself giving them a right to compensation." Robertson calls it "a pathetic question of hopeless lack of comprehension."

The Reformed Calvin reads Peter as "tacitly compar[ing] himself and the other disciples to the man whom the world had turned aside from Christ"; their inquiry is proper, "for it would be unreasonable if, after having been stripped of their property by the Lord, they should not be restored to a better condition." Yet "they err in this respect, that they demand a triumph to be given them, before they have finished their warfare." When we feel such impatience, "let us learn first to reflect on the comforts by which the Lord soothes the bitterness of the cup in this world, and next elevate our minds to the hope of the heavenly life." The witnesses agree: Peter's all was small and his motive mixed, yet Christ will answer his question with a generous promise (vv.28-29) even as he corrects its spirit (v.30).
28:
"Ye which have followed me, in the regeneration when the Son of man shall sit in the throne of his glory, ye also shall sit upon twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel." The witnesses divide over the timing and sense of "the regeneration." The grammatical-historical Barnes takes it of "that great revolution... that universal new birth when the dead shall rise... the day of judgment," the thrones meaning honour and rank: the apostles "shall be honoured by him... as having laid the foundations of his church." Lightfoot, however, reads it of the renewal wrought by the gospel: "renovation... is not of the body or substance of the world; but... consists in the renewing of the manners, doctrine," as Christ taught Nicodemus (John 3:3). Clarke surveys both the millennial and the gospel-age interpretations, preferring to refer "sitting on thrones" to "obtaining eternal salvation, and the distinguishing privileges of the kingdom of glory."

Ellicott reads "a new birth for mankind as well as for the individual" — "the times of the restitution of all things" (Acts 3:21) — in which the Twelve "should be foremost among the saints of the Most High" (Daniel 7:27), their names "on the twelve foundations of the heavenly Jerusalem" (Revelation 21:14). He frankly notes the promise was "given subject to the implied conditions of faithfulness and endurance," for "the guilt of Judas left one of the thrones vacant." Robertson cautions: "We must put no fantastic ideas into the mouth of Jesus. But he did look for the final consummation."

The Latin fathers read it variously. Hilary takes "the regeneration" as baptism, which "the Apostles followed, and which the Law could not bestow." Augustine reads "twelve thrones" as the fullness of all who judge — Paul too, who asks "Know ye not that we shall judge angels?" (1 Corinthians 6:3) — and "twelve tribes" as all the judged. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary refers "the regeneration" to the age of the Church, "the calling of the Gentiles," in which "every Christian who receives the word of Peter becomes Peter's throne."

The Reformed Calvin takes "the regeneration" of "the first coming of Christ," when "the world began to be renewed," but locates the thrones at "the last day," when the apostles "shall be partakers of the same glory." Wesley notes the implied condition: "the throne of Judas another took, so that he never sat thereon." The witnesses agree on the substance — supreme honour for the faithful apostles in Christ's triumphant kingdom — while differing on whether "regeneration" names the gospel age, the resurrection, or the renewed cosmos.
29:
"And every one that hath forsaken houses, or brethren... or lands, for my name's sake, shall receive an hundredfold, and shall inherit everlasting life." The witnesses note that the promise widens beyond the Twelve to all believers, and they explain the "hundredfold" spiritually. Barnes: "In our times it is not often required that we should literally leave them... but it is always required that we love them less than we do him." The hundredfold "is not to be understood literally, but that he will give what will be worth a hundred times as much, in the peace, and joy, and rewards of religion"; and Mark's added "with persecutions" shows these come "amidst their trials." Robertson notes the better reading "manifold," and that "eternal life is the real reward."

Ellicott draws out both the motive and the strange addition. The act "must not originate in a far-sighted calculation of reward; it must proceed from devotion to a Person and a cause" — hence the variants "for my sake and the gospel's" (Mark) and "for the kingdom of God's sake" (Luke). The hundredfold cannot be literal — "we cannot look for the hundredfold of houses, or wives" — but means "the spirit of insight and self-sacrifice... multiplies and intensifies even the common joys of life. Relationships multiply on the ground of spiritual sympathies... The common things of life — sky, and sea, and earth — are clothed with a new beauty." And Mark's "with persecutions" is "so strange that [it] must have been actually spoken... in a tone of tender sadness," reminding the disciples that "persecution in some shape... is inseparable from the higher life of the kingdom" (Acts 14:22).

The Reformed Calvin meets the objection from experience — that those stripped for Christ often remain "in exile, solitude, and desertion." He answers that "the immediate grace of God, by which he relieves the sorrows of his people," is "justly preferred to all the riches of the world"; the wicked, though prosperous, "must be always tossed about in perplexity and terror," while "God gladdens his people, so that the small portion of good which they enjoy is more highly valued... than if out of Christ they had enjoyed an unlimited abundance." Clarke names "the fullness of Grace here, and the fullness of Glory hereafter!" and Wesley extends it "to every one — in every age and country; not you my apostles only." The witnesses agree: every sacrifice for Christ is repaid a hundredfold in spiritual good now, amid persecutions, and crowned with eternal life.
30:
"But many that are first shall be last; and the last shall be first." The witnesses read this closing line as a corrective to the disciples' reckoning and a hinge to the parable that follows. The grammatical-historical Barnes points forward: "This verse should have been connected with the following chapter. The parable there spoken is expressly to illustrate this sentiment" (Matthew 20:16). Ellicott draws the personal application: the words point "not only to the general fact of the ultimate reversal of human judgments, but to the individual case of which the disciples had made themselves the judges." They had seen "one who stood high in his own estimate brought low," and were "flattering themselves that they, who had left all... were among the first in the hierarchy of the kingdom. For them too, unless their spirit should become other than it was in its self-seeking and self-complacence, there might be an unexpected change of position." The parable of the labourers "was designed to bring that truth more vividly before them."

Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) reads it redemptive-historically: "The Jews, who have been the first and most distinguished people of God, will in general reject the Gospel... The Gentiles, who have had no name among the living, shall be brought to the knowledge of the truth, and become the first." That "this prediction... has been literally fulfilled, the present state of the Christian and Jewish Churches sufficiently proves." Gill applies it both to the rich ruler — "first in this world... should be the last in the world to come" — and more widely.

The puritan Henry (under v.23) had drawn the same lesson: "pre-eminence in glory [goes not] by precedence in time, rather than the measure and degree of grace. No; many that are first shall be last... God will cross his hands; will reveal that to babes which he hid from the wise and prudent; will reject unbelieving Jews and receive believing Gentiles. The heavenly inheritance is not given as earthly inheritances commonly are, by seniority of age... but according to God's pleasure." Ellicott's closing warning gathers the witnesses: "Let us not trust in promising appearances or outward profession. Others may, for aught we know, become eminent in faith and holiness." The verse thus seals the chapter — from the empty self-confidence of the rich ruler to the mixed motives of the disciples — with the reminder that God's final reckoning will overturn every human estimate of rank.
